# Copilot instructions — reversi-py

Purpose
- Short guide for Copilot-style agents to work productively in this repository: how to run/build/test, high-level architecture, and repository-specific conventions.

1) Build / test / lint commands (how to run a single test)
- Recommended environment: Python 3.14.2+ and the `uv` helper (used in CI). Install uv: `curl -LsSf https://astral.sh/uv/install.sh | sh`.

Common commands (use `uv run ...` when available):
- Install deps (recommended): `uv sync` (or `uv sync --all-extras` to include server extras)
- Run the app: `uv run python main.py` or `python main.py`
- Run the full test suite: `uv run pytest tests/`
- Run a single test file: `uv run pytest tests/test_board.py -q`
- Run a single test case: `uv run pytest tests/test_board.py::test_place_stone -q`
- Run tests with coverage: `uv run pytest tests/ --cov=. --cov-report=term-missing --cov-report=json --cov-config=.coveragerc`
- Initialize GUI integration check: `uv run python scripts/test_gui_init.py` (on CI this is run under Xvfb)
- Lint (ruff): `uv run ruff check . --select=E,W,F --ignore=E501 --exclude=tests`
- Type check (mypy): `uv run mypy . --ignore-missing-imports`
- Local CI wrapper: `./scripts/ci_check.sh` (runs lint, mypy, pytest, coverage checks)

Notes on running a single test:
- Use pytest node ids: `pytest path/to/test_file.py::test_name -q` (prefix with `uv run` if using uv-managed environment).

2) High-level architecture (big picture)
- board.py: core Reversi rules and board state (is_valid_move, place_stone, get_flipped_stones).
- game.py: game state and orchestration (turn management, history, agents mapping, replay/history functions).
- agents/: AI agents (base_agent.py, first_agent.py, random_agent.py, gain_agent.py, mcts_agent.py, api_agent.py).
- gui.py + main.py: pygame-based GUI and App class. App.run manages the main loop: event handling -> state update -> render. AI runs in background threads and communicates via a queue.
- config/: agent definitions (agents_config.py), i18n, theme, and agent param helpers.
- server/: optional API server (api_server.py) and server-related tests.
- scripts/: CI helpers (ci_check.sh), GUI init test (test_gui_init.py), coverage checks (check_coverage.py).
- tests/: extensive unit tests (agents/, config/, server/, GUI tests). CI runs GUI init under Xvfb on Linux runners.

3) Key conventions specific to this repo
- Player/turn encoding: `-1` == Black, `1` == White. Many APIs use this numeric convention.
- Agent IDs: `0` == human player. Other integer IDs map to agent classes/params via `config/agents_config.py` and `config/agent_config_utils.py`. To add a new agent: implement agent class in agents/, then register its ID and params in config.
- Dependency manager: many scripts and CI use the `uv` wrapper. Commands in docs and CI assume `uv run <tool>`; prefer this when reproducing CI locally.
- Logging: `utils/logging_utils.ThrottlingFilter` is used to reduce repeated log spam. The App code attaches it to the root logger.
- Coverage policy: CI enforces a high coverage bar. The workflow requires 99%+ (excluding GUI). The repo contains helper scripts that validate coverage (scripts/check_coverage.py and CI checks).
- GUI tests: GUI-related tests require a virtual X server (Xvfb) on CI. `scripts/test_gui_init.py` exists to validate pygame/font initialization in headless CI.
- Ruff/mypy config: see `pyproject.toml` — ruff excludes `tests` and ignores E501; mypy is configured with ignore_missing_imports for smoother CI runs.
- Packaging: pyproject.toml lists packages = ["agents","config","server"]. Keep package layout aligned when adding modules.

Other repository notes (for agents/assistant integration)
- No CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, CONVENTIONS.md, or other assistant-specific rule files were found. (Search performed.)
- CI is configured in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`; it installs system packages necessary for building pygame on Linux (SDL/freetype) and runs the same `uv`-driven commands listed above.

If edits to this file are desired, say which sections to expand (examples, specific test names, or Python venv/pip instructions).
